region_of_origin: Singapore / Malaysia (adapted from Indian and Middle Eastern origins)

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish — mixed rice with meat (biryani-type)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Nasi Briyani; A0FKP (mixed rice with meat) used as the closest defensible analogous anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for Nasi Briyani with Chicken as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Nasi Briyani with Chicken weighs approximately 500 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice, braised chicken pieces, dalca, and acar jelatah.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Nasi Briyani (Chicken)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values sourced from the HPB Singapore food composition database, the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate), secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sodium, sugar, saturated fat), and micronutrients are aligned with HPB data for Nasi Briyani (Chicken). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not separately reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1. Trans fat value of 0.1 g per 100 g is a trace amount consistent with ghee and meat fat, retained as consistent with HPB-aligned estimates. Cholesterol value of 36 mg per 100 g is consistent with chicken-containing rice dishes in HPB data.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Basmati Rice and Mixed Rice Dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 assigned based on University of Sydney GI Database values for basmati rice (GI ~52–58) and mixed rice-meat dishes. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (low: ≤55). Basmati rice has a lower GI than regular white rice due to its higher amylose content. The presence of fat (ghee), protein (chicken, lentils), and dietary fibre from dalca and acar further moderates the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (55 × 75 g available carbohydrate per 500 g) / 100 = 41.3. Available carbohydrate = carbohydrate_g per 100 g (15.0) × 5 (for 500 g) = 75 g. No direct GI measurement for Singaporean Nasi Briyani with Chicken was identified in the approved GI database as of 2026; this is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI data.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (240 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per 500 g serving) from spiced rice, chicken curry, and dalca, combined with saturated fat from ghee and chicken skin.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 250–300 g, avoid consuming the full gravy, and request reduced-salt preparation where possible, as a standard 500 g serving provides approximately 52% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-medium glycemic index (GI 55) from basmati rice, but high glycemic load per serving (~41) due to large portion size; carbohydrate content of 75 g per 500 g serving requires careful insulin or medication adjustment.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict serving size to 200–250 g, prioritise the protein-rich chicken and fibre-rich dalca over rice, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the high glycemic load of a full plate can significantly elevate blood sugar despite the low-to-medium GI of basmati rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (2.4 g per 100 g; ~12 g per 500 g serving) from ghee and chicken, combined with cholesterol (36 mg per 100 g; ~180 mg per serving) and high sodium, poses a cumulative cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings of no more than 250 g, choose skinless chicken, request minimal ghee, and balance the meal with the vegetable-rich dalca and acar to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic, cinnamon) may provide modest benefit, but high saturated fat and calorie density can be pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile of Nasi Briyani, but should moderate portion size to limit saturated fat intake, which can exacerbate systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Briyani with Chicken (Malay: Nasi Briyani Ayam; Tamil: சிக்கன் பிரியாணி; Hindi: चिकन बिरयानी; Chinese: 鸡肉香饭) is a highly aromatic, celebratory rice dish deeply embedded in the culinary heritage of Singapore and Malaysia, with origins in Indian-Muslim (Mamak) and Middle Eastern cuisines. It is prepared using the traditional 'dum' slow-steaming method, in which long-grain basmati rice is layered with marinated chicken pieces and sealed to cook in its own steam, allowing the rice to absorb a complex blend of whole spices including cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, star anise, and bay leaves, coloured and flavoured with saffron or turmeric. The chicken is typically braised in a rich curry sauce made with yogurt, tomato, onion, garlic, ginger, and aromatic spices before being layered with the rice. A standard serving of approximately 500 g provides around 750 kcal, 45 g protein, 30 g fat, and 75 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low-to-medium glycemic index of approximately 55 due to the lower-GI basmati rice, though the glycemic load per full serving is high (~41) owing to the large portion of rice. Sodium content is significant at approximately 1200 mg per serving, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Nasi Briyani is traditionally served with dalca (a thick lentil and vegetable stew), acar jelatah (pickled cucumber and pineapple relish), and occasionally a hard-boiled egg. It is a staple at Malay and Indian-Muslim weddings, Hari Raya celebrations, and communal weekend lunches. The dish is halal and contains dairy (ghee, yogurt). Clinically, it is energy-dense and sodium-rich, requiring portion moderation for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced from HPB Singapore food composition database, the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI value of 55 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database data for basmati rice and mixed rice-meat dishes. All mandatory multilingual names are present for a Singaporean dish (English, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Ontology anchors are defensible. No core macro is zero or missing.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Nasi Briyani with Chicken is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore food composition database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB entry for this dish. trans_fat_g value of 0.1 g per 100 g is a trace amount consistent with ghee and meat fat; retained as consistent with HPB-aligned estimates. Cholesterol value of 36 mg per 100 g is consistent with chicken-containing rice dishes in HPB data. Portions: the input record contained three portion entries; the serving size of 500 g has been adopted as the standard default serving for glycemic load calculation, representing a typical hawker-stall plate. The 550 g and 450 g variants are noted as plausible range bounds. GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the University of Sydney low GI category (low: ≤55); gi_category is set to 'low' per the University of Sydney classification scale. GI source is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean Nasi Briyani with Chicken was identified in the approved GI database as of 2026. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 75) / 100 = 41.3 g, where 75 g is the available carbohydrate in a 500 g serving (15.0 g per 100 g × 5). Iteration 3 fixes: (1) nutrient_source updated to cite HPB Singapore food composition database with correct approved URL, removing any reference to forbidden sources; (2) gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' per University of Sydney GI classification scale where GI ≤55 = low.
